content: {"answer": "The most directly relevant source is a trial-based and lifetime-model cost-utility study of a fasting-mimicking diet programme in type-2-diabetes patients (Sources 1, 4, 13). That study determined cost-effectiveness of a one-year monthly 5-consecutive-day fasting-mimicking-diet programme added to diabetes standard care, and is identified as 'the first to evaluate the costs and consequences of adding the FMD fasting nutri-technology to diabetes standard care' (Source 13). However, specific ICER values were not captured in the snippets provided. For general lifestyle/weight-loss comparators: AJMC (Source 3) reports non-pharmacologic diet-and-exercise interventions with QALY cost ranging from $25,400 to $57,400, indicating good value; the NEW Soul Study (Source 6) reported two-year weight loss of 8% and cost per QALY gained of $1,704-$7,889; NICE economic model (Source 7) reported cost per QALY of \u00a316,794 for a dietary intervention vs standard care; a telehealth intervention (Source 11) reported passive-intervention ICER of $7,084/QALY under $50,000 willingness-to-pay; DPP interventions (Source 12) ranged from $8,800 to $62,600 per QALY depending on Markov vs Archimedes model. No direct head-to-head cost-effectiveness comparison of IF counseling vs usual care, structured CER, or GLP-1 pharmacotherapy is present in the snippets. The FMD-for-T2D cost-utility study is the closest to a formal IF cost-effectiveness analysis; no published ICER for 16:8 TRE or 5:2 IF in primary-care weight management surfaced. Missing information: direct ICER for IF counseling, side-by-side comparison with GLP-1 pharmacotherapy (semaglutide/tirzepatide) which would dominate opportunity-cost calculations, sensitivity analyses to adherence decay, and payer-perspective analyses.", "key_findings": ["One trial-based + lifetime-model cost-utility analysis exists for a fasting-mimicking diet programme in T2D patients (Sources 1, 4, 13) but specific ICERs are not captured in available snippets.", "Non-pharmacologic diet/exercise QALY cost ranges $25,400-$57,400 (AJMC, Source 3) \u2014 baseline for cost-effective lifestyle interventions.", "The Nutritious Eating with Soul Study (Source 6): 8% weight loss at 2 years, $1,704-$7,889 per QALY gained.", "NICE dietary intervention cost per QALY \u00a316,794 (Source 7); telehealth passive $7,084/QALY (Source 11).", "No head-to-head cost-effectiveness comparison of IF counseling vs GLP-1 pharmacotherapy exists in the retrieved set."], "sources_used": ["https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12599020/", "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/397459835_Cost-effectiveness_of_a_periodic_fasting-mimicking_diet_programme_in_patients_with_type_2_diabetes_a_trial-based_analysis_and_a_lifetime_model-based_analysis", "https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1098301524028468", "https://www.ajmc.com/view/cost-effectiveness-of-varying-weight-loss-interventions-elena-losina-phd", "https://scholarcommons.sc.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=7414&context=etd", "https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng246/evidence/economic-model-report-pdf-13620245869", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11638481/", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2954575/"]}
